title: Iteractive maps
text:
Code
# Initial packages required (we'll be adding more)
library(tidyverse)
library(mdsr)      # package associated with our MDSR book
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(htmltools)
library(glue)
library(rvest)
library(polite)

title: Iteractive maps
section: 2.1 Car Fatality Rates: A Numeric Portrait
text:
2.1 Car Fatality Rates: A Numeric Portrait
The first variable I explored is carfatal07 – the number of traffic fatalities per 100,000 people in 2007. This isn’t just a number; behind each data point is a story of infrastructure quality, rural vs. urban geography, speed limits, and even cultural attitudes about driving.


Code
# Define the geojson URL
us_geojson <- "https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson"

# Code from OYO #1 in 09_table_scraping.qmd

session <- bow("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density", force = TRUE)

result <- scrape(session) |>
  html_nodes(css = "table") |> 
  html_table(header = TRUE, fill = TRUE)
density_table <- result[[1]]
density_table


# A tibble: 61 × 6
   Location               Density Density Population `Land area` `Land area`
   <chr>                  <chr>   <chr>   <chr>      <chr>       <chr>      
 1 Location               /mi2    /km2    Population mi2         km2        
 2 District of Columbia   11,131  4,297   678,972    61          158        
 3 New Jersey             1,263   488     9,290,842  7,354       19,047     
 4 Rhode Island           1,060   409     1,095,962  1,034       2,678      
 5 Puerto Rico            936     361     3,205,691  3,424       8,868      
 6 Massachusetts          898     347     7,001,399  7,800       20,202     
 7 Guam[4]                824     319     172,952    210         543        
 8 Connecticut            747     288     3,617,176  4,842       12,542     
 9 U.S. Virgin Islands[4] 737     284     98,750     134         348        
10 Maryland               637     246     6,180,253  9,707       25,142     
# ℹ 51 more rows


Code
density_data <- density_table |>
  select(1, 2, 4, 5) |>
  filter(!row_number() == 1) |>
  rename(Land_area = `Land area`) |>
  mutate(state_name = str_to_lower(as.character(Location)),
         Density = parse_number(Density),
         Population = parse_number(Population),
         Land_area = parse_number(Land_area)) |>
  select(-Location)

density_data


# A tibble: 60 × 4
   Density Population Land_area state_name            
     <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>                 
 1   11131     678972        61 district of columbia  
 2    1263    9290842      7354 new jersey            
 3    1060    1095962      1034 rhode island          
 4     936    3205691      3424 puerto rico           
 5     898    7001399      7800 massachusetts         
 6     824     172952       210 guam[4]               
 7     747    3617176      4842 connecticut           
 8     737      98750       134 u.s. virgin islands[4]
 9     637    6180253      9707 maryland              
10     578      43915        76 american samoa[4]     
# ℹ 50 more rows


Code
# Load the state geometry
states_sf_alt <- st_read(us_geojson, quiet = TRUE) |>
  mutate(state_name = str_to_lower(name))

# Join the data
states_final <- states_sf_alt |>
  left_join(density_data, by = "state_name") |>
  filter(!(name %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii", "Puerto Rico")))  # Remove non-contiguous

# Define the color palette 
bins <- c(0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, Inf)
pal <- colorBin("YlOrRd", domain = states_final$Density, bins = bins)

# Create the map
leaflet(states_final) |>
  setView(-96, 37.8, 4) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal(Density),
    weight = 2,
    opacity = 1,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    label = ~glue("{name}: {Density} people/sq mi"),
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(weight = 5, bringToFront = TRUE)
  ) |>
  
  addLegend(pal = pal, values = ~Density, title = "Population Density<br>(people/sq mi)")







The states dataset in the poliscidata package contains 135 variables on each of the 50 US states. See here for more detail. The task here is to create a two meaningful choropleth plots, one using a numeric variable and one using a categorical variable from poliscidata::states.



Code
# Get info to draw US states for geom_polygon (connect the lat-long points)
library(maps)
states_polygon <- as_tibble(map_data("state")) |>
  select(region, group, order, lat, long)

# See what the state (region) levels look like in states_polygon
unique(states_polygon$region)


 [1] "alabama"              "arizona"              "arkansas"            
 [4] "california"           "colorado"             "connecticut"         
 [7] "delaware"             "district of columbia" "florida"             
[10] "georgia"              "idaho"                "illinois"            
[13] "indiana"              "iowa"                 "kansas"              
[16] "kentucky"             "louisiana"            "maine"               
[19] "maryland"             "massachusetts"        "michigan"            
[22] "minnesota"            "mississippi"          "missouri"            
[25] "montana"              "nebraska"             "nevada"              
[28] "new hampshire"        "new jersey"           "new mexico"          
[31] "new york"             "north carolina"       "north dakota"        
[34] "ohio"                 "oklahoma"             "oregon"              
[37] "pennsylvania"         "rhode island"         "south carolina"      
[40] "south dakota"         "tennessee"            "texas"               
[43] "utah"                 "vermont"              "virginia"            
[46] "washington"           "west virginia"        "wisconsin"           
[49] "wyoming"             


Code
# Get info to draw US states for geom_sf and leaflet (simple features object 
#   with multipolygon geometry column)
library(sf)
states_sf <- 
  read_sf("https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/json/us-states.geojson") |>
  select(name, geometry)

# See what the state (name) levels look like in states_sf
unique(states_sf$name)


 [1] "Alabama"              "Alaska"               "Arizona"             
 [4] "Arkansas"             "California"           "Colorado"            
 [7] "Connecticut"          "Delaware"             "District of Columbia"
[10] "Florida"              "Georgia"              "Hawaii"              
[13] "Idaho"                "Illinois"             "Indiana"             
[16] "Iowa"                 "Kansas"               "Kentucky"            
[19] "Louisiana"            "Maine"                "Maryland"            
[22] "Massachusetts"        "Michigan"             "Minnesota"           
[25] "Mississippi"          "Missouri"             "Montana"             
[28] "Nebraska"             "Nevada"               "New Hampshire"       
[31] "New Jersey"           "New Mexico"           "New York"            
[34] "North Carolina"       "North Dakota"         "Ohio"                
[37] "Oklahoma"             "Oregon"               "Pennsylvania"        
[40] "Rhode Island"         "South Carolina"       "South Dakota"        
[43] "Tennessee"            "Texas"                "Utah"                
[46] "Vermont"              "Virginia"             "Washington"          
[49] "West Virginia"        "Wisconsin"            "Wyoming"             
[52] "Puerto Rico"         


Code
# Load in state-wise data for filling our choropleth maps
#   (Note that I selected my two variables of interest to simplify)
library(poliscidata)   # may have to install first
polisci_data <- as_tibble(poliscidata::states) |>
  select(state, carfatal07, cook_index3)

# See what the state (state) levels look like in polisci_data
unique(polisci_data$state)   # can't see trailing spaces but can see


 [1] Alaska                                    
 [2] Alabama                                   
 [3] Arkansas                                  
 [4] Arizona                                   
 [5] California                                
 [6] Colorado                                  
 [7] Connecticut                               
 [8] Delaware                                  
 [9] Florida                                   
[10] Georgia                                   
[11] Hawaii                                    
[12] Iowa                                      
[13] Idaho                                     
[14] Illinois                                  
[15] Indiana                                   
[16] Kansas                                    
[17] Kentucky                                  
[18] Louisiana                                 
[19] Massachusetts                             
[20] Maryland                                  
[21] Maine                                     
[22] Michigan                                  
[23] Minnesota                                 
[24] Missouri                                  
[25] Mississippi                               
[26] Montana                                   
[27] NorthCarolina                             
[28] NorthDakota                               
[29] Nebraska                                  
[30] NewHampshire                              
[31] NewJersey                                 
[32] NewMexico                                 
[33] Nevada                                    
[34] NewYork                                   
[35] Ohio                                      
[36] Oklahoma                                  
[37] Oregon                                    
[38] Pennsylvania                              
[39] RhodeIsland                               
[40] SouthCarolina                             
[41] SouthDakota                               
[42] Tennessee                                 
[43] Texas                                     
[44] Utah                                      
[45] Virginia                                  
[46] Vermont                                   
[47] Washington                                
[48] Wisconsin                                 
[49] WestVirginia                              
[50] Wyoming                                   
50 Levels: Alabama                                    ...


Code
                             #   lack of internal spaces
print(polisci_data)   # can see trailing spaces


# A tibble: 50 × 3
   state                                        carfatal07 cook_index3
   <fct>                                             <dbl> <fct>      
 1 "Alaska                                    "       15.2 More Rep   
 2 "Alabama                                   "       25.9 More Rep   
 3 "Arkansas                                  "       23.7 More Rep   
 4 "Arizona                                   "       17.6 Even       
 5 "California                                "       11.7 More Dem   
 6 "Colorado                                  "       12.3 Even       
 7 "Connecticut                               "        8.7 More Dem   
 8 "Delaware                                  "       13.6 More Dem   
 9 "Florida                                   "       18.1 Even       
10 "Georgia                                   "       18.5 Even       
# ℹ 40 more rows




Code
# Make sure all keys have the same format before joining:
#   all lower case, no internal or external spaces

states_polygon <- states_polygon |>
  mutate(region = str_squish(str_to_lower(region)))

states_sf <- states_sf |>
  mutate(name = str_squish(str_to_lower(name)))

polisci_data <- polisci_data |>
  mutate(state = str_squish(str_to_lower(state)))

# Join first, then filter to contiguous states
contiguous_states <- c("alabama", "arizona", "arkansas", "california", "colorado", 
                      "connecticut", "delaware", "florida", "georgia", "idaho", 
                      "illinois", "indiana", "iowa", "kansas", "kentucky", 
                      "louisiana", "maine", "maryland", "massachusetts", "michigan", 
                      "minnesota", "mississippi", "missouri", "montana", "nebraska", 
                      "nevada", "new hampshire", "new jersey", "new mexico", 
                      "new york", "north carolina", "north dakota", "ohio", 
                      "oklahoma", "oregon", "pennsylvania", "rhode island", 
                      "south carolina", "south dakota", "tennessee", "texas", 
                      "utah", "vermont", "virginia", "washington", "west virginia", 
                      "wisconsin", "wyoming")

# Join all data first
states_polygon_merged <- states_polygon |>
  left_join(polisci_data, by = c("region" = "state")) |>
  filter(region %in% contiguous_states)  # Filter AFTER join

# Now check - should be 48
n_distinct(states_polygon_merged$region)


[1] 48




Code
# Now we can merge data sets together for the static and the interactive plots

# Merge with states_polygon (static)
states_polygon_merged <- states_polygon |>
  left_join(polisci_data, by = c("region" = "state")) |>
  filter(region %in% contiguous_states)

# Check that merge worked for 48 contiguous states
n_distinct(states_polygon_merged$region)  # Should be 48


[1] 48


Code
unique(states_polygon_merged$region) 


 [1] "alabama"        "arizona"        "arkansas"       "california"    
 [5] "colorado"       "connecticut"    "delaware"       "florida"       
 [9] "georgia"        "idaho"          "illinois"       "indiana"       
[13] "iowa"           "kansas"         "kentucky"       "louisiana"     
[17] "maine"          "maryland"       "massachusetts"  "michigan"      
[21] "minnesota"      "mississippi"    "missouri"       "montana"       
[25] "nebraska"       "nevada"         "new hampshire"  "new jersey"    
[29] "new mexico"     "new york"       "north carolina" "north dakota"  
[33] "ohio"           "oklahoma"       "oregon"         "pennsylvania"  
[37] "rhode island"   "south carolina" "south dakota"   "tennessee"     
[41] "texas"          "utah"           "vermont"        "virginia"      
[45] "washington"     "west virginia"  "wisconsin"      "wyoming"       


Code
# Merge with states_sf (static or interactive)
states_sf_merged <- states_sf |>
  left_join(polisci_data, by = c("name" = "state")) |>
  filter(name %in% contiguous_states)

# Check that merge worked for 48 contiguous states
n_distinct(states_sf_merged$name)  


[1] 48


Code
unique(states_sf_merged$name) 


 [1] "alabama"        "arizona"        "arkansas"       "california"    
 [5] "colorado"       "connecticut"    "delaware"       "florida"       
 [9] "georgia"        "idaho"          "illinois"       "indiana"       
[13] "iowa"           "kansas"         "kentucky"       "louisiana"     
[17] "maine"          "maryland"       "massachusetts"  "michigan"      
[21] "minnesota"      "mississippi"    "missouri"       "montana"       
[25] "nebraska"       "nevada"         "new hampshire"  "new jersey"    
[29] "new mexico"     "new york"       "north carolina" "north dakota"  
[33] "ohio"           "oklahoma"       "oregon"         "pennsylvania"  
[37] "rhode island"   "south carolina" "south dakota"   "tennessee"     
[41] "texas"          "utah"           "vermont"        "virginia"      
[45] "washington"     "west virginia"  "wisconsin"      "wyoming"       


Numeric variable (static plot):


Code
ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = states_polygon_merged, 
               aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, 
                   fill = carfatal07),
               color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient(name = "Car Fatalities (2007)",
                     low = "#fee5d9", high = "#a50f15",
                     na.value = "grey90") +
  coord_map() +
  theme_minimal()


Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.









What the static map reveals: The deep red clusters across the South – Mississippi, West Virginia, Alabama, and South Carolina – tell a troubling story. These states consistently show fatality rates above 20 per 100,000 people. In contrast, the Northeast and Upper Midwest appear in lighter shades, with Massachusetts and Minnesota showing rates below 10. The pattern suggests a strong rural-urban divide: states with larger rural populations and longer driving distances tend to have higher death rates. Montana and Wyoming are notable exceptions – despite being rural, their fatality rates are moderate, likely due to lower population density and different highway designs.
Data source: poliscidata package (2007 car fatality rates per 100,000 population)
Numeric variable (interactive plot):


Code
# it's okay to skip a legend here
pal_numeric <- colorNumeric(
  palette = "Reds",
  domain = states_sf_merged$carfatal07
)

leaflet(states_sf_merged) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal_numeric(carfatal07),
    weight = 1,
    opacity = 1,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(
      weight = 3,
      color = "#666",
      fillOpacity = 0.9
    ),
    label = ~paste0(name, ": ", round(carfatal07, 1), " fatalities/100k"),
    labelOptions = labelOptions(
      style = list("font-weight" = "normal", padding = "3px 8px"),
      textsize = "12px",
      direction = "auto"
    )
  )






Interactive exploration: Hover over any state to see its exact fatality rate. Notice how the gradient intensifies as you move from the Northeast down through Appalachia into the Deep South. You can also see how states along the same interstate corridors (like I-95 or I-40) sometimes have dramatically different safety records depending on which side of a state line you’re on.

title: Iteractive maps
section: 3.1 Cook Partisan Voting Index: A Categorical View
text:
3.1 Cook Partisan Voting Index: A Categorical View
The Cook PVI measures how strongly a state leans Democratic or Republican compared to the national average. Rather than treating politics as a binary (red vs. blue), the index reveals degrees of competition. A rating of “Even” means the state is a true battleground – the kind of place where presidential elections are decided.
Categorical variable (static plot):


Code
# be really careful with matching color order to factor level order
states_polygon_merged <- states_polygon_merged |>
  mutate(cook_cat = factor(cook_index3, 
                          levels = c("More Dem", 
                                    "Even", 
                                    "More Rep"),
                          ordered = TRUE))

ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(data = states_polygon_merged, 
               aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group, 
                   fill = cook_cat),
               color = "white", size = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(name = "Cook PVI",
                    values = c("More Dem" = "#08519c",
                              "Even" = "#f7f7f7",
                              "More Rep" = "#cb181d"),
                    na.value = "grey90",
                    drop = FALSE) +
  coord_map() +
  theme_minimal()









What the static map reveals: The pattern is striking. Democratic-leaning states cluster along the Pacific Coast (California, Oregon, Washington), the Northeast corridor (New York, Massachusetts, Connecticut), and around the Great Lakes (Illinois, Michigan). Republican-leaning states dominate the South, the Plains, and the Mountain West. But the most interesting category is “Even” – these battleground states (Colorado, Florida, Iowa, Nevada, New Hampshire, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Virginia, Wisconsin) form a geographic belt across the middle of the country. This is where presidential campaigns spend millions of dollars for a reason.
Data source: poliscidata package (Cook Partisan Voting Index)


Code
polisci_data|>
  count(cook_index3)


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  cook_index3     n
  <fct>       <int>
1 More Rep       17
2 Even           18
3 More Dem       15


Categorical variable (interactive plot):


Code
# may use colorFactor() here

states_sf_merged <- states_sf_merged |>
  mutate(cook_cat = factor(cook_index3, 
                          levels = c("More Dem", 
                                    "Even", 
                                    "More Rep"),
                          ordered = TRUE))

# Define color palette matching the order
cat_colors <- c("More Dem" = "#08519c",
                              "Even" = "#f7f7f7",
                              "More Rep" = "#cb181d")

pal_categorical <- colorFactor(
  palette = cat_colors,
  domain = states_sf_merged$cook_cat,
  ordered = TRUE
)

leaflet(states_sf_merged) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal_categorical(cook_cat),
    weight = 1,
    opacity = 1,
    color = "white",
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(
      weight = 3,
      color = "#666",
      fillOpacity = 0.9
    ),
    label = ~paste0(name, ": ", cook_index3),
    labelOptions = labelOptions(
      style = list("font-weight" = "normal", padding = "3px 8px"),
      textsize = "12px",
      direction = "auto"
    )
  ) |> addLegend(pal = pal_categorical, values = ~cook_cat, title = "Cook PVI")






Interactive exploration: Click around the map to see how the political landscape shifts. Notice that “Even” states aren’t random – they form a corridor from Virginia to Colorado. Also observe how some states we think of as reliably partisan actually have competitive scores: Texas leans Republican but not as strongly as Oklahoma or Wyoming.

title: Netflix Text Analysis
text:
#Introduction: What Stories Is Netflix Telling Us?
Netflix has revolutionized how we consume entertainment, but have you ever wondered how the streaming giant describes its content? The words chosen to summarize movies and TV shows aren’t random—they’re carefully crafted to attract specific audiences while accurately representing the content.
In this analysis, I dive deep into Netflix’s title descriptions to uncover:
What words appear most frequently?
How does sentiment differ across content?
Do TV ratings (TV-MA, TV-14, PG, etc.) use different language?
What happens when negation words like “not” or “never” flip the meaning?
Let’s explore what 8,000+ Netflix descriptions reveal about how we talk about entertainment.
#Uploading the data to the environment
#Data tyding
Just a for fun: By running this code we can see the number of movies vs TV Shows that we have in the data. Later on in this project I will be looking at specific show/movie descriptions, however I will look at them combined and not differentiate if some words are for movie or TV show descriptions. Here we have 6131 movies and 2676 TV Shows.


Code
Netflix_Data <- read_csv("C://Users//komba1//Downloads//Leas data set.csv") 


Rows: 8807 Columns: 12
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (11): show_id, type, title, director, cast, country, date_added, rating,...
dbl  (1): release_year

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.


Code
Netflix_Data|>
  count(type)


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  type        n
  <chr>   <int>
1 Movie    6131
2 TV Show  2676


#Data tidying


Code
tidy_netflix <- Netflix_Data |>
  mutate(line = row_number()) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, description, token = "words")


#The Big Picture
Most Common Words in Netflix Descriptions Before diving into sentiment, let’s look at the words Netflix uses most frequently to describe its content:


Code
tidy_netflix |>
  anti_join(get_stopwords(source = "smart")) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  filter(word != "NA") |>
  slice_max(n, n = 20) |>
  ggplot(aes(fct_reorder(word, n), n)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip()


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`





The top 20 most common words in the descriptions of Netflix shows and movies have amongst them words such as life, deatch, father, love, woman, friends, etc. We can see here that the most common word in this very simple analysis is life , and the least common word out of the top 20 is father.




#The Emotional Palette
Positive vs. Negative Words Using the Bing sentiment lexicon, I classified words as positive or negative to see the emotional balance:


Code
get_sentiments(lexicon = "afinn")


# A tibble: 2,477 × 2
   word       value
   <chr>      <dbl>
 1 abandon       -2
 2 abandoned     -2
 3 abandons      -2
 4 abducted      -2
 5 abduction     -2
 6 abductions    -2
 7 abhor         -3
 8 abhorred      -3
 9 abhorrent     -3
10 abhors        -3
# ℹ 2,467 more rows


Code
get_sentiments(lexicon = "nrc")


# A tibble: 13,872 × 2
   word        sentiment
   <chr>       <chr>    
 1 abacus      trust    
 2 abandon     fear     
 3 abandon     negative 
 4 abandon     sadness  
 5 abandoned   anger    
 6 abandoned   fear     
 7 abandoned   negative 
 8 abandoned   sadness  
 9 abandonment anger    
10 abandonment fear     
# ℹ 13,862 more rows


Code
get_sentiments(lexicon = "bing")


# A tibble: 6,786 × 2
   word        sentiment
   <chr>       <chr>    
 1 2-faces     negative 
 2 abnormal    negative 
 3 abolish     negative 
 4 abominable  negative 
 5 abominably  negative 
 6 abominate   negative 
 7 abomination negative 
 8 abort       negative 
 9 aborted     negative 
10 aborts      negative 
# ℹ 6,776 more rows


Code
bing_sentiments <- get_sentiments(lexicon = "bing")

tidy_netflix |>   
  inner_join(bing_sentiments) |>
  count(sentiment)


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`


Warning in inner_join(tidy_netflix, bing_sentiments): Detected an unexpected many-to-many relationship between `x` and `y`.
ℹ Row 115374 of `x` matches multiple rows in `y`.
ℹ Row 504 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.


# A tibble: 2 × 2
  sentiment     n
  <chr>     <int>
1 negative  11465
2 positive   7919


Here we can see that from the most common words in this dataset, 11465 have a negative sentiment and 7919 have a positive sentiment.


Code
#| fig.alt: "This barplot shows the distribution of the most common words in the Netflix dataset based on their sentimemt (positive or negative). In the x-axis we have the number of times these words appear ranging from 0 to 200 (negative sentiment) and 0 to 500 (positive sentiment), and the y-axis represents the top 10 most common words, ranging from most common to least common; and the graph is wrapped based on the sentiment."

tidy_netflix |>
  inner_join(bing_sentiments,relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(sentiment, word, sort = TRUE) |>
  group_by(sentiment) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(word, n), y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
    geom_col() +  
    coord_flip() +
    facet_wrap(~ sentiment, scales = "free")


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`





From the top 20 most common words in the descriptions of Netflix shows and movies, we split them in half showing the top 10 words with negative or positive sentiment. Words such as murder, mysterious and crime have negative sentiment, whreas words like best, work and love have positive sentiment. From the words with negative sentiment, murder is the one that shows up the most whereas dangerous shows up the least. For words with positive sentiment, love is the one that appears more often and modern appears the least. Based on the range on the Y-axis for this plot, we can observe something interesting: the fact that the graph that represents negativ sentiment has a y-axis ranging from 0 - 200 while the graph with positive sentiment ranges from 0 - 500, can tell us that overall in this data set, words with positive sentiment appear more often.




According to what we can see in this wordclout , these are the words that are more often mentioned in descriptions of netflix shows. The larger the word size, the more often it appears.


Code
##whch ones are positive and which ones are negative?
library(reshape2)



Attaching package: 'reshape2'


The following object is masked from 'package:tidyr':

    smiths


Code
tidy_netflix %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing")) %>%
  count(word, sentiment, sort = TRUE) %>%
  acast(word ~ sentiment, value.var = "n", fill = 0) %>%
  comparison.cloud(colors = brewer.pal(6, "Dark2"),
                   max.words = 100)


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`

ℹ Row 504 of `y` matches multiple rows in `x`.
ℹ If a many-to-many relationship is expected, set `relationship =
  "many-to-many"` to silence this warning.









#Beyond Single Words
The Power of Bigrams Single words can be misleading. For example, “love” might appear in “love story” (positive) or “love gone wrong” (negative). That’s why I analyzed bigrams—two-word combinations to capture context:


Code
netflix_ngram <- Netflix_Data |>
  unnest_tokens(bigram, description, token = "ngrams", n = 2) |>
  filter(bigram != "NA")

netflix_ngram


# A tibble: 204,283 × 12

Code
netflix_ngram |>
  count(bigram, sort = TRUE)

   bigram      n

Code
##Let's remove stop words
netflix_bigrams_filtered <- netflix_ngram |>
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") |>
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word,
         !word2 %in% stop_words$word) |>
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE)
netflix_bigrams_filtered

#visualizing bigrams in a network


Code
#| fig.cap: "From the most common paired words we can see in this network plot, how often and which words are paired in this dataset. The largest network, is the one that connects crime->organized to family->life_>changing."

#| fig.alt: "This network plot shows the connection between paired words, showing of the most common words combined with negation words in the Netflix dataset based on their sentimemt (positive or negative). In the x-axis we have the sentiment score of these words  ranging from -8 to 3  and the y-axis we have the words that follow a specific negation word. "

library(igraph)

# filter for only relatively common combinations
bigram_graph <- netflix_bigrams_filtered |>
  filter(n > 10) |>
  graph_from_data_frame()

bigram_graph

+ ... omitted several edges


Code
# Use ggraph to convert into a network plot
library(ggraph)
set.seed(2017)

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point() +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), vjust = 1, hjust = 1)









Code
# polish the graph
set.seed(2020)
a <- grid::arrow(type = "closed", length = unit(.15, "inches"))

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(edge_alpha = n), show.legend = FALSE,
                 arrow = a, end_cap = circle(.07, 'inches')) +
  geom_node_point(color = "lightpink", size = 5) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), vjust = 1, hjust = 1) +
  theme_void()









#Bigrams sentiments
Now lets look at the context in which these combination of words appear so that we can see if they actually contribute to a negative or positive sentiment.


Code
netflix_bigrams_afinn <- netflix_ngram |>
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") |>
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word,
         !word2 %in% stop_words$word) |>
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE) |>
  
  # Add AFINN scores
  left_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = c("word1" = "word")) |>
  rename(afinn_word1 = value) |>
  left_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = c("word2" = "word")) |>
  rename(afinn_word2 = value) |>
  
  # Create combined sentiment columns
  mutate(
    # Replace NA with 0
    afinn_word1 = replace_na(afinn_word1, 0),
    afinn_word2 = replace_na(afinn_word2, 0),
    
    # Combined scores
    combined_sum = afinn_word1 + afinn_word2,
    
    
    # Sentiment category based on combined score
    combined_sentiment_afinn = case_when(
      combined_sum > 2 ~ "very positive",
      combined_sum > 0 ~ "positive",
      combined_sum == 0 ~ "neutral",
      combined_sum < 0 ~ "negative",
      combined_sum < -2 ~ "very negative"
    )
  )

# View results
netflix_bigrams_afinn |>
  select(word1, word2, n, afinn_word1, afinn_word2, 
         combined_sum, combined_sentiment_afinn) |>
  filter(afinn_word1 != 0 | afinn_word2 != 0) |>
  head(20)

 1 true  story    57           2           0            2 positive              
 2 world war      53           0          -2           -2 negative              
 3 war   ii       45          -2           0           -2 negative

 5 civil war      26           0          -2           -2 negative

10 award winn…    17           3           4            7 very positive         
11 free  spir…    17           1           2            3 very positive         
12 orga… crime    14           0          -3           -3 negative              
13 car   acci…    13           0          -2           -2 negative              
14 love  life     13           3           0            3 very positive         
15 resc… miss…    13           2           0            2 positive

17 top   secr…    12           2           0            2 positive              
18 come… seri…    11           1           0            1 positive              
19 fun   lovi…    11           4           2            6 very positive         
20 war   torn     11          -2          -2           -4 negative              
# ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​combined_sentiment_afinn


#The Negation Effect: When “Not” Changes Everything
One of the most fascinating aspects of language is how negation words flip meaning. “Good” is positive, but “not good” is negative. Conversely, “bad” is negative, but “not bad” is positive.
Let’s see how Netflix uses negation:


Code
netflix_afinn <- get_sentiments("afinn")

bigrams_separated <- netflix_ngram |>
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") |>
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE) |>
  filter(!is.na(word1) & !is.na(word2))

bigrams_separated |> filter(word1 == "not")


# A tibble: 49 × 3
   word1 word2       n
   <chr> <chr>   <int>
 1 not   be         11
 2 not   to          8
 3 not   knowing     7
 4 not   so          6
 5 not   the         5
 6 not   all         3
 7 not   just        3
 8 not   as          2
 9 not   even        2
10 not   have        2
# ℹ 39 more rows


Code
not_words <- bigrams_separated |>
  filter(word1 == "not") |>
  inner_join(netflix_afinn, by = c(word2 = "word")) |>
  arrange(desc(n))

not_words


# A tibble: 5 × 4
  word1 word2       n value
  <chr> <chr>   <int> <dbl>
1 not   accept      1     1
2 not   admit       1    -1
3 not   alone       1    -2
4 not   easy        1     1
5 not   solving     1     1




Code
#| fig.cap: "From the most common words that are often accompained by a negation word (no, not, never, without), we can see that the words that come after the negation word never, when combined have a more negative sentiment, whereas words that come after the negation word without, often tend to a slighty positive sentiment."

#| fig.alt: "This barplot shows the distribution of the most common words combined with negation words in the Netflix dataset based on their sentimemt (positive or negative). In the x-axis we have the sentiment score of these words  ranging from -8 to 3  and the y-axis we have the words that follow a specific negation word. "

# An example of expanding the list of negation words
negation_words <- c("not", "no", "never", "without")

negated_words <- bigrams_separated |>
  filter(word1 %in% negation_words) |>
  inner_join(netflix_afinn, by = c(word2 = "word")) |>
  arrange(desc(n))

negated_words


# A tibble: 20 × 4
   word1   word2         n value
   <chr>   <chr>     <int> <dbl>
 1 no      nonsense      5    -2
 2 no      matter        3     1
 3 no      prisoners     2    -2
 4 never   dull          1    -2
 5 never   failing       1    -2
 6 never   missed        1    -2
 7 never   suspects      1    -1
 8 never   won           1     3
 9 no      easy          1     1
10 no      help          1     2
11 no      honor         1     2
12 no      interest      1     1
13 no      justice       1     2
14 not     accept        1     1

16 not     alone         1    -2
17 not     easy          1     1
18 not     solving       1     1
19 without justice       1     2
20 without prospects     1     1


Code
negated_words |>
  mutate(contribution = n * value) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(contribution))) |>
  group_by(word1) |>
  slice_max(abs(contribution), n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(word2 = reorder(word2, contribution)) |>
  ggplot(aes(n * value, word2, fill = n * value > 0)) +
    geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
    facet_wrap(~ word1, scales = "free") +
    labs(x = "Sentiment value * number of occurrences",
         y = "Words preceded by negation term")









#Rating Matters: How Language Differs by TV Rating
Now for the most revealing part of my analysis: How does language change across different TV ratings?
I filtered out duration-based ratings (like “90 min”) and focused on content ratings: TV-MA, TV-14, TV-PG, R and PG-13.
#Bigrams: Word Pairs by Rating


Code
# First, let's create a bigram dataset with ratings and sentiment
bigrams_with_ratings <- Netflix_Data |>
  filter(!str_detect(rating, "^\\d+ min$")) |>
  filter(rating %in% c("R", "TV-14","PG-13","TV-MA","TV-PG"))|>
  mutate(line = row_number()) |>
  unnest_tokens(bigram, description, token = "ngrams", n = 2) |>
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ", remove = FALSE) |>
  filter(!is.na(word1) & !is.na(word2)) |>
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word & !word2 %in% stop_words$word) |>
  
  # Add sentiment for both words using AFINN
  left_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = c("word1" = "word")) |>
  rename(afinn_word1 = value) |>
  left_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = c("word2" = "word")) |>
  rename(afinn_word2 = value) |>
  
  # Calculate combined sentiment
  mutate(
    afinn_word1 = replace_na(afinn_word1, 0),
    afinn_word2 = replace_na(afinn_word2, 0),
    combined_score = afinn_word1 + afinn_word2,
    bigram_sentiment = case_when(
      combined_score > 0 ~ "positive",
      combined_score < 0 ~ "negative",
      TRUE ~ "neutral"
    )
  )

# Now count bigrams by rating and sentiment
bigrams_by_rating <- bigrams_with_ratings |>
  count(rating, bigram, bigram_sentiment, sort = TRUE) |>
  group_by(rating) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 5) |>  # Top 5 bigrams per rating
  ungroup()

# Plot
bigrams_by_rating |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(bigram, n), y = n, fill = bigram_sentiment)) +
    geom_col() +
    coord_flip() +
    facet_wrap(~ rating, scales = "free_y") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("positive" = "darkgreen", 
                                  "negative" = "darkred", 
                                  "neutral" = "gray50")) +
    labs(
      title = "Most Common Bigrams by TV Rating",
      subtitle = "Colored by combined AFINN sentiment score",
      x = "Bigrams", 
      y = "Frequency",
      fill = "Sentiment"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      strip.text = element_text(size = 8, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7)
    )





When analysing how the paired words are distributed across the selected TV rating types, we can see that for R and TV-PG the TV-Shows or Movies have most often paired words with negative or neutral sentiment, whereas for PG-13 and TV-14 we see most often neutral themes.




#Unigrams: Single Words by Rating


Code
tidy_netflix |>
  filter(!str_detect(rating, "^\\d+ min$")) |>
  filter(rating %in% c("R", "TV-14","PG-13","TV-MA","TV-PG"))|>
  inner_join(bing_sentiments, relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(rating, word, sentiment, sort = TRUE) |>
  group_by(rating) |>
  slice_max(n, n = 5) |>  # Top 5 words per rating
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(word, n), y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
    geom_col() +
    coord_flip() +
    facet_wrap(~ rating, scales = "free_y") +
    labs(
      title = "Most Common Words by TV Rating",
      x = "Words", 
      y = "Frequency",
      fill = "Sentiment"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      strip.text = element_text(size = 8, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom"
    )


Joining with `by = join_by(word)`





When analysing how single words are distributed across the selected TV rating types, we can see that for R, TV-MA and TV-14 the TV-Shows or Movies have most often single words with negative sentiment, whereas for PG-13 and TV-PG we see most often positive sentiment




#Negation Patterns Across Ratings Finally, let’s see how negation words behave differently across content ratings:


Code
negated_words_by_rating <- Netflix_Data |>
  filter(!str_detect(rating, "^\\d+ min$")) |>
    filter(rating %in% c("R", "TV-14","PG-13","TV-MA","TV-PG"))|>

  mutate(line = row_number()) |>
  unnest_tokens(bigram, description, token = "ngrams", n = 2) |>
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ", remove = FALSE) |>
  filter(!is.na(word1) & !is.na(word2)) |>
  filter(word1 %in% negation_words) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"), by = c("word2" = "word")) |>
  count(rating, word1, word2, value, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(contribution = n * value)

negated_words_by_rating |>
  group_by(rating, word1) |>
  slice_max(abs(contribution), n = 5) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(word_label = paste(word1, word2, sep = " → ")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = rating, y = fct_reorder(word_label, contribution), fill = contribution)) +
    geom_tile() +
    scale_fill_gradient2(
      low = "darkred", 
      mid = "white", 
      high = "darkgreen",
      midpoint = 0,
      name = "Contribution"
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "Negation Word Pairs Across Ratings",
      subtitle = "Green = positive context after negation, Red = negative context after negation",
      x = "Rating", 
      y = "Negation → Word pairs"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() 









#What have we Learned About Netflix’s Language?
Based on this comprehensive analysis of over 8,000 Netflix titles, we can conclude that Netflix’s description language reveals a sophisticated content strategy that balances emotional appeal with audience targeting. While the raw counts show more negative words (11,465) than positive words (7,919) overall, positive terms like “love,” “best,” and “work” appear more frequently.
The most revealing finding here is how language shifts dramatically by content rating: mature-rated content (R, TV-MA, TV-14) relies heavily on negative sentiment words associated with crime, violence, and psychological tension, while youth-oriented ratings (PG-13, TV-PG) feature more positive and neutral language centered on friendship and adventure. In addition the analysis of negation patterns further demonstrates that words following “never” tend toward negative sentiment while those after “without” lean positive, showing how Netflix uses context to create nuanced descriptions that manage viewer expectations.
Ultimately, these patterns reveal that Netflix’s descriptions are carefully crafted linguistic tools that not only summarize content but strategically signal genre conventions, emotional journeys, and appropriate audiences through deliberate word choices and contextual framing.

title: Lea Komba
text:
Dear Visitor,
Thank you for checking out my website. My name is Lea Komba, and I am a Statistics & Data Science , and Political Science Double major at St.Olaf College (Class of 2028). Uff! That might seem like a lot to read lead alone to do, but these two majors perfectly combine both my interests in social trends and visualizing data.
Outside of academics, I am a social entrepreneur, currently co-founding and running two non-profit organizations in Angola, southern Africa, one is Biso, a social hub that focuses on providing tools and mentorship for the next generation of young change makers in Angola, and the other one id Soppel, which focuses on providng academic mentorship for colleges aged students looking for scholarships abroad. Other than that, I love music and dancing, so invite to a nice concert or live performance anytime.
